nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, heavily-vegetated margins of slow-moving or standing waters; including Pandanus-lined creeks with muddy bottoms, sago forest rivulets, drainage ditches, and billabongs

decorPreferences: Dense planting along sides and back for cover and spawning sites
Floating plants to diffuse light and provide surface refuge
Fine-leaved aquatic moss (e.g. Taxiphylum sp.) for egg attachment
Driftwood roots or branches consistent with a swamp/forest biotope
Open mid-water and surface swimming space
Dark or natural substrate to bring out coloration
Gentle filtration — sponge filter or spray-bar directed at tank wall preferred

diet.notes: A micro-carnivore that feeds primarily on small zooplankton and invertebrates. Generally does best on live and frozen micro-foods: Daphnia, Moina, Artemia nauplii, microworms, and rotifers. Small crushed flake or micro-granule dry foods are typically accepted but most specimens tend to show better condition and coloration on a live-food-heavy diet. Small portions offered multiple times daily are generally advisable given the species' tiny stomach capacity.

avoidWith: Larger or boisterous fish that outcompete for food
Any fish large enough to outcompete the blue-eyes at feeding time

summary: Gertrude's Spotted Blue Eye (Pseudomugil gertrudae) is one of the smallest members of the rainbowfish group, known for its iridescent blue eyes and spotted finnage. Males develop extended dorsal and anal fins and are more vividly colored. Best kept in calm, planted tanks in groups of 8 or more.
careNotes:
Among the smallest members of the Pseudomugil genus and the rainbowfish family broadly. Males develop noticeably extended and spotted dorsal and anal fins as they mature, and engage in fascinating competitive displays that are one of the highlights of keeping this species. Wild populations across northern Australia, Papua New Guinea, and the Aru Islands tend to show highly localized differences in coloration, body size, and fin morphology — most aquarium specimens available in the hobby are reportedly derived from a single commercial strain whose exact origin is unclear.

Best kept in groups of 8 or more to encourage natural shoaling behavior and male display. Smaller groups often result in shy, reclusive fish. The species is generally considered peaceful but is quite easily outcompeted at feeding time by larger or more assertive tankmates; a species-appropriate community or nano tank setup is usually advisable. Pairs well with shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) and similarly-sized Pseudomugil species such as the Forktail Blue-eye (Pseudomugil furcatus) and Threadfin Rainbowfish (Iriatherina werneri).

The species is sensitive to water chemistry fluctuations and is generally not recommended for biologically immature tanks. Water quality should be maintained consistently; ammonia and nitrite spikes that hardier species might tolerate tend to cause visible distress. Good oxygenation and some water movement are advisable even though very strong currents should be avoided. Lifespan in captivity has been reported to be relatively short — females in particular tend to become less fecund after 12–18 months, and wild populations may only survive a single reproductive season. Well-maintained aquarium conditions have been reported to extend lifespan somewhat beyond wild norms.

The species occupies the upper water column and surface. A tightly-fitting lid or low water level is advisable as these fish have been reported to jump.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer exhibiting no parental care. Spawning is typically triggered by temperatures toward the upper end of the range (24–28°C) and has been reported to follow a seasonal cycle linked to the onset of the wet season in the wild (October–December). Eggs are deposited in small numbers daily over several days, attached to fine-leaved aquatic moss or spawning mops. Incubation is approximately 10 days depending on temperature. Fry are very small on hatching and generally require microscopic foods such as Paramecium for the first several days before graduating to Artemia nauplii; small meals offered at least twice daily tend to support faster growth. Growth rate has been reported to be comparatively fast relative to some other Pseudomugil species. Using a dedicated breeding tank or removing eggs to a separate rearing vessel tends to improve yield, as adults may consume eggs in a community setting.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, native range, water parameters, tank size, diet, temperament, grouping, tankmate recommendations, breeding details, and care notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pseudomugilidae, Atheriniformes), native range (northern Australia, southern New Guinea, Aru Islands), adult size (3.8 cm SL), temperature (23–30°C). Temperature range averaged across SeriouslyFish (21–28°C) and FishBase (23–30°C) to produce a 22–28°C aquarium range consistent with user brief. Hardness confirmed at 5–12 dH consistent with SeriouslyFish. FishBase pH range (5.2–6.7) is narrower than SeriouslyFish (4.5–7.5); aquarium range set to 6.0–7.5 per user brief and the broader husbandry-recommended window.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Pseudomugil care similarities. Article confirms that Pseudomugil gertrudae shares broadly similar care requirements to the Forktail Blue-eye (P. furcatus). Used for tankmate and grouping confirmation.

fish.breedingNotes: Gertrude's Spotted Blue Eye is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs on fine-leaved plants or moss over several days. Spawning is achievable in a well-maintained aquarium, but raising the fry successfully requires microscopic live foods in the early stages and attentive husbandry throughout.
